title: 9 Tahsas — St. Samuel & The Assembly
narrative:
The ninth day of Tahsas honors the Prophet Samuel, whose name in the Hebrew is interpreted as 'heard by God' or 'name of God,' for his mother Hannah prayed for him in bitter supplication and the Lord heard her cry, granting her a son whom she consecrated entirely to the divine service. Samuel grew up in the sanctuary at Shiloh under the priest Eli and received his first prophetic call in the night watches, when the voice of the Lord spoke his name three times in the darkness and he at last responded, 'Speak, Lord, for Thy servant heareth.' He became the last of the judges and the first of the great prophets, anointing Saul and David as kings of Israel, establishing the prophetic office as a check upon royal power, and typifying in his intercessory prayers the high-priestly intercession of the coming Messiah.

His mother Hannah the Prophetess, whose name means 'grace' or 'favor,' stands beside her son in this day's commemoration, for her prayer of thanksgiving recorded in the first book of Samuel — 'My heart rejoiceth in the Lord; mine horn is exalted in the Lord' — is read by the fathers as a prophetic canticle that the Virgin Mary consciously echoed in her Magnificat. Hannah sang of the barren woman made fruitful, of the mighty brought low and the humble exalted, of the Lord who raises the poor from the dust and sets him among princes; and in singing these things she sang of what would come to pass in the fullness of time when another woman, overshadowed by the Most High, would sing 'He hath regarded the lowliness of His handmaiden.'

The Conception of Mary — that is, the feast commemorating the conception of the Virgin Mary in the womb of her mother Anne — is observed on this day in the tradition that informs the Ethiopian Synaxarium. This mystery, distinct from the dogma of the Immaculate Conception as defined in the Western Church, celebrates the providential preparation of the vessel of the Incarnation: God, foreknowing that He would take flesh from the Virgin, prepared her from the very moment of her conception in her mother's womb, sanctifying her and adorning her with graces befitting the Mother of the Lord.

The theological seal of this ninth day of Tahsas is the mystery of the answered prayer. Hannah prayed in silence and was heard; Samuel learned to hear the divine voice in the night; Mary was conceived as the answer to all the prayers of Israel for the consolation that was to come. This day teaches that the Incarnation is not an arbitrary divine intervention but the fulfilment of a prayer that creation has been uttering since its very beginning — the groan of all flesh for the living God, answered at last in the flesh of the Virgin's Son.
